sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: ACTION7.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 36, level 2.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 18 of about 42 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
ACTION7 reset everything! The squares are back to their original positions:
- M: (26-27, 9-10) - wait, this is square 1 which I moved earlier
- M: (36-40, 40-41) - square 2, back to original
- M: (53-54, 48-49) - square 3, back to original
- M: (54-55, 13-14) - square 4, back to original
Wait, square 1 is at (26-27, 9-10), not the original (35-36, 16-17). So ACTION7 didn't fully reset - it only reset square 2 which was on the blob.
Actually, let me re-check. The original positions were:
1. (35-36, 16-17)
2. (36-37, 40-41)
3. (53-54, 48-49)
4. (54-55, 13-14)
After ACTION7:
1. (26-27, 9-10) - NOT original
2. (36-37, 40-41) - ORIGINAL
3. (53-54, 48-49) - ORIGINAL
4. (54-55, 13-14) - ORIGINAL
So ACTION7 reset square 2 (which was on the blob) back to its original position, but didn't reset square 1 (which I moved earlier but wasn't on the blob). This is interesting!
So ACTION7 seems to reset squares that are ON the blob back to their original positions. This means the goal is probably NOT to move squares onto the blob (since ACTION7 undoes that).
Wait, but ACTION7 also changed the board (gameplay_changed=True). Let me reconsider what ACTION7 does.
Actually, maybe ACTION7 is an "undo" or "reset" action. It undoes the last move. Let me check: before ACTION7, square 2 was at (30-31, 34-35) (on the blob). After ACTION7, square 2 is back at (36-37, 40-41) (original position). This looks like an undo!
But square 1 is still at (26-27, 9-10), not at its original (35-36, 16-17). So ACTION7 only undoes the LAST move, not all moves.
Wait, but I made many moves before moving square 2 to the blob. Let me trace back:
- I moved square 1 multiple times (to 33-34, 15-16, then 31-32, 13-14, then 31-32, 14-15, then 28-29, 11-12, then 26-27, 9-10)
- I moved square 2 to (30-31, 34-35)
- Then I called ACTION7
If ACTION7 is undo, it should undo the last action, which was moving square 2 to (30-31, 34-35). And indeed, square 2 went back to (36-37, 40-41).
But wait, the last action before ACTION7 was MOUSE(row=31, col=35), which moved square 2. So ACTION7 undid that. But square 1's moves were earlier, so they weren't undone.
So ACTION7 is UNDO! It undoes the last action.
This changes my strategy. I've been wasting actions by moving squares and then undoing. Let me focus on the actual goal.
Given that:
1. Squares can be moved by clicking cells (the clicked cell becomes the bottom-right of the nearest square)
2. ACTION7 undoes the last action
3. The blue blob is in the center
4. There's a yellow square at the top
Maybe the goal is to move all 4 magenta squares to specific positions, possibly matching the colored squares in the legend (S, M, p, Y).
Looking at the legend:
- S (sky blue) at (1-2, 1-2)
- M (magenta) at (1-2, 5-6)
- p (purple) at (1-2, 9-10)
- Y (yellow) at (1-2, 13-14)
And there's a yellow 4x4 square at (3-6, 30-33).
Maybe the goal is to move the 4 M squares to form a pattern, or to move them to specific target positions.
Actually, let me reconsider the level. The legend has 4 colors: S, M, p, Y. There are 4 M squares on the board. Maybe each M square needs to be moved to a position where it changes to the corresponding color in the legend.
Or maybe the goal is simpler: move all 4 M squares to overlap with the blue blob, and they'll change color.
Let me try moving one square to the blob and see if it changes color. But I need to be careful not to waste too many actions.
Actually, I realize I've used 18 actions already out of ~42. I need to be much more efficient. Let me try to move all 4 squares to the blob in 4 clicks (one per square), then see what happens.
Current M squares:
1. (26-27, 9-10)
2. (36-37, 40-41)
3. (53-54, 48-49)
4. (54-55, 13-14)
Blob: rows 23-31, cols 29-37
For each square, I need to click a position within the blob that's closest to that square:
1. Square 1 (26-27, 9-10): click (27, 29) → moves to (26-27, 28-29)
2. Square 2 (36-37, 40-41): click (31, 37) → moves to (30-31, 36-37)
3. Square 3 (53-54, 48-49): click (29, 37) → moves to (28-29, 36-37)
4. Square 4 (54-55, 13-14): click (31, 31) → moves to (30-31, 30-31)
But the problem is that after moving one square, it becomes the nearest to subsequent clicks. I need to order the clicks carefully.
Let me think about the order. I should move the farthest squares first, clicking positions that are closest to them but far from already-moved squares.
Actually, let me try a different approach. What if I click positions that are NOT in the blob, but rather positions that are 1-2 cells away from where I want the square to end up? This way, the square moves closer to the blob but not all the way there. Then I can make a second click to move it onto the blob.
Hmm, this is getting complicated. Let me just try moving all 4 squares to the blob in 4 clicks and see what happens. If the nearest-square logic causes problems, I'll adjust.
Actually, I just realized something. The squares might not all need to go to the blob. Maybe the goal is to move them to match the legend colors. The legend has S, M, p, Y. Maybe:
- One M square needs to become S (sky blue)
- One M square stays M (magenta)
- One M square needs to become p (purple)
- One M square needs to become Y (yellow)
And the way to change a square's color is to move it to a specific location (like the blob for one color, the yellow square for another, etc.).
Let me look at the board more carefully. There's:
- A blue blob in the center
- A yellow 4x4 square at the top (3-6, 30-33)
- 4 M squares scattered around
Maybe each M square needs to be moved to a different target:
- One to the blue blob → becomes blue
- One to the yellow square → becomes yellow
- etc.
But I only see 2 targets (blue blob and yellow square), not 4. Unless the legend squares are also targets.
OK, I'm overthinking this. Let me just try moving all 4 squares to the blob and see what happens. I'll batch the clicks.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# ACTION7 is UNDO - it undoes the last action
# Let me focus on the actual goal: move all 4 M squares to the blue blob
# 
# Current M squares:
# 1. (26-27, 9-10)
# 2. (36-37, 40-41)
# 3. (53-54, 48-49)
# 4. (54-55, 13-14)
#
# Blob: rows 23-31, cols 29-37
#
# Strategy: move farthest squares first, clicking positions in the blob
# that are closest to each target square

# Plan the clicks carefully to ensure the right square is nearest:
# Click 1: Move square 3 (53-54, 48-49) - farthest
# Click at (29, 37) - top-right of blob, closest to square 3
# Click 2: Move square 4 (54-55, 13-14) - second farthest  
# Click at (31, 29) - bottom-left of blob, closest to square 4
# Click 3: Move square 1 (26-27, 9-10) - third farthest
# Click at (27, 33) - top-center of blob, closest to square 1
# Click 4: Move square 2 (36-37, 40-41) - closest to blob
# Click at (31, 35) - bottom-center of blob

# But I need to verify nearest-square logic after each move...
# Let me just try and see

# First, let me move square 3 (farthest) to blob
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 29, 'col': 37}])
print(f"Click 1: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
m_squares = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 4]
print("M squares:")
for n in m_squares:
    print(f"  {n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
